summary: All 28 TDD Anchor cases from task-3 are present and behaviourally meaningful across the four test files (6/6 TapCounter, 5/5 LogcatReader, 3/3 DeviceInfoCollector, 14/14 LogExportManager). Robolectric setup mirrors ErrorLogTest canonically: RobolectricTestRunner + @Config(sdk=[33]) + ApplicationProvider + symmetric @Before/@After with logsDir.deleteRecursively(); no Mockito/MockK — only hand-rolled StubCommandRunner, RecordingCommandRunner, ThrowingCommandRunner, ThrowingOutputStream, StubDeviceInfoProvider. TapCounterTest correctly omits Robolectric (pure JVM) and feeds virtual time via a mutable closure. The argv-shape test locks all six positions including the `^--pid=\d+$` regex for argv[4] — OWASP A03 guardrail solid. Section-ordering is verified by indexOf comparison with per-section substringing and per-marker contains checks (not a flat 'all dividers appear somewhere'). Truncation tests verify head/tail preservation with distinctive Greek/ASCII markers plus directional placement of the `[truncated: head ... bytes]` / `[truncated at 100 KB]` marker, and bound section bytes ≤ 100KB + 128 overhead. writeTo_nullOutputStream asserts on the exact exception message 'openOutputStream returned null'. The non-Hilt construction test exercises the secondary `LogExportManager(Context)` constructor directly with an empty filesDir and CrashReport source; since CrashReport skips logcat by Decision 8, DefaultCommandRunner's ProcessBuilder path is not hit at runtime but is still instantiated in the secondary ctor — the guardrail catches any future `internal` regression at compile time. The extra test writeTo_happyPath_writesContent is valuable (not bloat): without it, every writeTo branch would be a negative-path test with no positive coverage that bytes actually land in the stream. Test independence is clean — no shared mutable state, fresh manager()/counter per test, logsDir wiped twice per LogExportManagerTest case. Three low-severity findings: the head-truncation test contains the marker but does not directionally anchor it to END of the section; the `[logcat unavailable: unknown]` branch (production code line 42, introduced beyond the TDD Anchor) is an uncovered extra branch; and the nonHiltConstruction test intentionally exercises only the CrashReport path, leaving the non-Hilt DefaultCommandRunner+LogcatReader construction path untouched at runtime (acceptable by Decision 8 design, but worth a comment). No critical/high/medium issues, no blockers.

findings.issue: crashLogHeadTruncation_preservesHeadAndMarks uses crashSection.contains("[truncated at 100 KB]") without a directional anchor. Production appends CRASH_TRUNC_MARKER = "\n[truncated at 100 KB]" AFTER the 100 KB head slice, so marker-at-end is a load-bearing invariant that the current assertion does not enforce. A regression that accidentally placed the marker at the start of the section (e.g. CRASH_TRUNC_MARKER + head) would still pass this test. The corresponding logcat test (line 154) is already directional (asserts marker is in the first 200 chars). This same finding was flagged in task-2 test-reviewer-1.json for stacktraceOver100KB_truncationMarkerAtEnd.
findings.recommendation: Add a directional anchor alongside the existing contains() check: `assertTrue("crash trunc marker must be at the end of the section, got last 100 chars:\n${crashSection.takeLast(100)}", crashSection.trimEnd().endsWith("[truncated at 100 KB]"))`. One extra line, closes the symmetry with the logcat-tail-truncation test which already anchors the marker to the head.

findings.issue: LogcatReader.read() has an `exitCode == null && !timedOut` branch (LogcatReader.kt line 42 → "[logcat unavailable: unknown]") that is not in the TDD Anchor and not covered by any test. This branch fires when DefaultCommandRunner catches IOException/SecurityException from ProcessBuilder.start() and returns LogcatResult(ByteArray(0), null, false). Currently the placeholder string "[logcat unavailable: unknown]" is only exercised by dead code — removing that branch or changing its text would not fail any test. Litmus test fails (replace the literal with anything else, suite still green).
findings.recommendation: Add a small test mirroring the timeout/exit=N pattern: `@Test fun unknownFailure_returnsUnavailableUnknown() { val runner = StubCommandRunner(LogcatResult(stdout = ByteArray(0), exitCode = null, timedOut = false)); val reader = LogcatReader(runner); assertEquals("[logcat unavailable: unknown]", reader.read()) }`. Five lines, closes the branch coverage gap and locks the placeholder text to the contract. Alternatively — remove the `unknown` branch from production if it is unreachable (but the IOException/SecurityException fallback in DefaultCommandRunner can produce exactly that state, so the branch is live).

findings.issue: nonHiltConstruction_instantiatesWithoutInjection only invokes buildExport(ExportSource.CrashReport), which skips LogcatReader by Decision 8. The secondary constructor instantiates DefaultCommandRunner() and LogcatReader(DefaultCommandRunner()) eagerly, so `internal`-downgrade or deleted-constructor regressions are caught at compile time — which the task ticks off as the primary guardrail. However, the runtime behaviour of the non-Hilt path through logcat is never exercised anywhere in the suite (CrashReport skips it, the Hilt path uses StubCommandRunner). If AndroidDeviceInfoProvider or DefaultCommandRunner ever acquired a runtime-breaking side effect in their public constructors (e.g. a static init that throws without Hilt's @ApplicationContext resolver), the current test could still pass because it never calls buildExport(About) via the non-Hilt path.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) document in a comment that this test is a compile-time guardrail by design and runtime coverage of the non-Hilt logcat path is deliberately left for instrumented tests (cheap, zero LoC), or (b) add a second test that calls `LogExportManager(realContext).buildExport(ExportSource.About)` and asserts the output contains `=== logcat ===` and either the runner's output or a `[logcat unavailable: ...]` placeholder — this exercises DefaultCommandRunner.run in Robolectric, which will return exit=nonzero or empty but will not throw, giving end-to-end runtime coverage of the non-Hilt path. Option (b) is ~10 LoC and closes the gap.
